Market Commentary

The S&P 500 fell 4.3% in the first quarter of 2025, resulting in the worst quarterly performance for U.S. equities in more than two years. Market volatility and downside pressure increased near the end of the quarter, in reaction to policy shifts and uncertainty around increased tariffs. Recent economic data indicated a slowing economy and weakening consumer confidence.

The first quarter saw a reversal in the performance of the “Magnificent Seven” with each of these large companies posting negative returns after their market-leading performance over the last two years. U.S. value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the first quarter, marking a substantial reversal from 2024. 2 While the valuation disparity between value and growth stocks narrowed, the Russell 1000 Value traded at 17.2 times forward earnings, a meaningful discount to the Russell 1000 Growth Index at 25.6 times forward earnings on March 31. 3

Portfolio Strategy

In the current uncertain economic climate, we are closely reviewing risks and opportunities and continue to take counter-cyclical actions to identify undervalued investments, consistent with our approach as active, value-oriented investors.

In early 2025, we added to holdings in the Industrials, Materials, Real Estate, and Health Care sectors, and trimmed select holdings in Financials, continuing a similar trend from last year. In 2024, the Health Care sector faced significant challenges due to margin pressures and concerns about the potential for adverse regulatory changes. After being 2024's largest detractor, Health Care was the top-contributing sector to the Fund’s relative performance during the first quarter of 2025. Our activity in the shares of CVS Health is an example of our contrarian, long-term approach. 4

CVS has rebounded strongly after weak 2024 performance, up over 50% in the first quarter. 2024 was a difficult year for CVS due to weaker sales at its pharmacies and higher medical costs in its Medicare Advantage health insurance segment. The company’s results rebounded in the fourth quarter under new CEO David Joyner, who joined in October. The strong results fueled investor hopes for a turnaround. Consistent with our contrarian approach, we added to CVS during 2024 and early 2025 to take advantage of the company’s depressed valuation and our positive long-term outlook for the company.

Recent Market Volatility

The Trump administration's tariff policy rollout in the first weeks of April increased fears of higher inflation and a possible recession, which led to increased market volatility. When market prices change, we revisit our investment theses security-by-security, incorporate recent developments, reassess our long-term outlook, and make incremental changes to the portfolio. We’ve navigated challenging economic periods in the past and have used them to uncover attractive long-term investment opportunities at compelling valuations. Our past experiences give us confidence in our investment approach and ability to serve our shareholders' long-term best interests through the market’s current turbulence.

We remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the Fund, which trades at a significant discount to the broader market and is diversified across sectors and investment themes. On March 31, the Fund traded at only 14.1 times forward earnings, compared to 20.9 times for the S&P 500 and 17.2 times for the Russell 1000 Value.

We encourage shareholders to take a long-term view and are here to answer any questions you may have.

Thank you for the continued confidence you’ve placed in Dodge & Cox.

data.title: BKCH: Lagging Peers And Overly Concentrated
data.description:
The Global X Blockchain ETF (NASDAQ:BKCH) is an actively managed fund that offers investors exposure to companies that stand to benefit from blockchain technology innovation. Some of the ways the fund builds that exposure is through publicly listed equities that touch blockchain technology either through digital asset transactions, cryptocurrency mining, mining hardware, or integration with blockchain networks. In my initial coverage of BKCH, we'll look at fund details compared to peers, holdings, and whether I personally think the ticker is one to consider for investment.

BKCH Details

There are really only a handful of ETFs that I have come across that have attempted to build a 'blockchain' fund as a theme. While this isn't an exhaustive list, I see the closest natural peers for BKCH as the Fidelity Crypto Industry and Digital Payments ETF (FDIG), the VanEck Digital Transformation ETF (DAPP), and the iShares Blockchain and Tech ETF (IBLC). The latter of which is the smallest at just $27.3 million in AUM.

Blockchain ETFs BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Issuer Global X Funds Fidelity Investments VanEck iShares
Inception 07/12/2021 04/19/2022 04/12/2021 04/25/2022
Expense Ratio 0.50% 0.40% 0.51% 0.47%
AUM $113,160,512 $132,467,749 $133,702,510 $27,296,737

Source: Seeking Alpha, AUM as of 4/15/25

Fee-wise, the funds are all very close though FDIG is cheapest. But at 50 basis points, I don't think BKCH is terribly overpriced for an actively managed fund. However, the fund is very concentrated in top stocks and only has about two dozen positions.

Blockchain ETFs BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Number of Holdings 25 53 22 48
Assets in Top 10 74.8% 68.5% 62.9% 66.4%
Turnover 56.4% 53.0% 57.0% 81.0%

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

The Global X Blockchain ETF has a similar turnover ratio to FDIG and DAPP at a little over 56% and has less holdings than FDIG at just 25. Perhaps the biggest thing that separates BKCH from the rest of the pack is the concentration in the fund's top 10 holdings. At just under 75% of assets in the top 10 holdings, this is a fund that I'd call reliant on its top stocks.

ETF Grades BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
Momentum F D- F F
Expenses C+ B- C+ C+
Dividends A- F D- C+
Risk F F F F
Liquidity C C+ B F

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

One thing that is interesting is the fund gets an 'A+' Dividend grade from Seeking Alpha while the other funds are well below that. At 11.5%, BKCH has a very high trailing twelve month yield. The TTM yield on DAPP is 6.3% and IBLC paid out 2.2% over the last year.

Performance vs The Peer Group

Since three of these funds are actively paying dividends, it's important to look at the performance of the funds through the total return rather than just the share price performance. From a total return standpoint, these ETFs have all had generally awful starts to 2025:

At a 34% negative total return, BKCH is closer to the worst performer of the peer group shown above. The 1-year performance is also quite poor with BKCH standing out as the clear laggard of the group:

With all of these funds generally sharing similar themes and 12 month trends, it ultimately comes down to where the major allocations are to discern what might explain the underperformance of BKCH relative to the other funds.

Holdings Breakdown

Before we get into the deeper BKCH breakdown, I wanted to look at the top 3 assets for each fund as well as the allocation to those top three positions. For ease of reading the table, I'm keeping it to just the tickers. The unique stocks that make it into these top 3 positions are the following:

  • Coinbase Global (COIN)
  • MARA Holdings (MARA)
  • Riot Platforms (RIOT)
  • CleanSpark (CLSK)
  • Strategy (MSTR)
  • Block, Inc (XYZ)

Unsurprisingly, there are a lot crypto-focused companies and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining stocks. Three of the funds have top equity exposure to Coinbase:

Top 3 Stocks BKCH FDIG DAPP IBLC
1st COIN COIN MSTR COIN
2nd MARA MARA COIN MARA
3rd RIOT CLSK XYZ RIOT
Top 3 Weighting 37.0% 25.0% 25.4% 30.8%

Source: Seeking Alpha, as of 4/15/25

I should note that this table puts COIN as FDIG's top position, but FDIG actually has 23.8% of AUM in the income-based Fidelity Securities Lending Cash Central Fund. Which could possibly disqualify that fund from consideration entirely since it might not make sense to pay Fidelity 40 basis points to put a quarter of the fund's assets in T-bills. The flip side of that coin is perhaps FDIG is better insulated from drawdowns - and we can certainly see that reflected in FDIG's relative outperformance year to date.

Here's the full asset breakdown for BKCH specifically:

57.4% of the fund is allocated to Bitcoin mining stocks. To me, that seems like a large allocation. For an actively managed fund, I think there can/should be more discernment regarding what is included in a 'blockchain' ETF. In my opinion, there are some fairly notable omissions from this fund that I would think would fit with the overall thesis of businesses using blockchain. Some examples of publicly traded companies that I'm surprised didn't make it in the fund would be Exodus Movement, Inc. (NYSE:EXOD), Fold Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:FLD), and Coincheck Group N.V. (NASDAQ:CNCK). Furthermore, Ethereum (ETH-USD) mining with GPUs produced by NVIDIA (NVDA) ended over 2.5 years ago. That BKCH has almost as much exposure to NVDA as it does to a company like Bit Digital (BTBT) seems off base for a 'blockchain' fund.

Closing Thoughts

I think investors who want exposure to 'blockchain' as an idea can probably do better picking their own stocks. Beyond direct buys of Coinbase, MARA Holdings, and maybe Galaxy Digital (GLXY:CA)(OTCPK:BRPHF), there are a lot of companies in this fund that investors can likely pass on if they want to play the 'blockchain' ponies. Even something like EXOD, which I'm not actually bullish, makes more thematic sense than some of the other companies included in BKCH. At the end of the day, it's a fund with a lot of capital allocated to a handful of crypto-adjacent stocks and a high reliance on a mining sector that faces long term economic challenges. For me, BKCH is a 'hold.'

data.title: Live Oak Bancshares: Bank On It Today
data.description:
Amid macroeconomic uncertainty in the US, banks may face some challenges in their profitability and sustainability. Yet, some exude confidence with their continued expansion, strategic business focus, and prudent asset management. One of them is Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. (NYSE:LOB). It was established at the height of the Great Recession, which shows that breakthroughs could be achieved even in the most challenging situations. With its stable topline performance and sound Balance Sheet, I believe the bank can still do well amid some headwinds. In addition, it’s cheap now and deserves to bounce back.

LOB Q4 2024: A Decent Year-End

Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. ended the year with a solid performance amid its continued expansion and increased exposure to SMBs.

Its interest income amounted to $212.6M, up by 14.0% YoY From $186.5M. We can note that growth has become somewhat toppish in the past six quarters. It just became flatter and more obvious during the second half of the year.

If we look at interest income on loans, it rose by 14.9% from Q4 2023. However, its difference from Q3 2024 was only 1.6%. The same can be seen in investment income since these are mostly bonds and mortgage-backed securities. We can attribute it to the sharp rate cut by the Fed during H2 2024. From August to September, it stashed it by 100 bps.

Nonetheless, LOB saw a stronger net interest income before provisions as it increased by 18.7% YoY and 2.8% QoQ to $115.1M. After all, interest rate cuts also led to lower interest expense on deposits and borrowings.

Considering all these aspects, I do believe LOB remains resilient amid the offsetting effect of the policy easing cycle on its interest income and expense. It is also worth noting that LOB gained more consumers’ and borrowers’ confidence, as shown by its increasing loan and deposit base. The fact that LOB sustained growth despite higher credit loss provisions shows that expansion stays fruitful. This can also show prudent management of its interest-sensitive assets and liabilities. I will discuss this more later.

Laggards

LOB is well-positioned today, but some risks are already on the horizon. Trade war poses the biggest threat to its performance as it can spur inflation and volatility in the long run. This can also make it hard for the Fed to continue its policy easing cycle or at least stabilize inflation and interest rates. This can affect the performance of LOB since macroeconomic uncertainties can lower confidence among its clients and borrowers. Loan repayment capacity and deposit base may also be affected, so it must be cautious.

Despite this, I hope that the falling Brent and WTI prices can mitigate these threats. The price drop can be supported by the OPEC oil supply increase in the next two months. If the trend continues, it can partially offset the impact of tariffs through lower fuel, transportation, and shipping expenses. This can help industries heavily affected by oil prices, such as airlines, public transportation, shipping, automobiles, restaurants, and freight and logistics. Hence, companies can absorb the impact of tariffs or lower the amount to be passed on to consumers to stabilize economic activities.

Drivers

Despite the potential risks, I think LOB will remain stable with these attributes.

Well-Positioned Loan/Deposit Base For Market Uncertainties

After the uptick to 3.00% last January, inflation slowed again to 2.8% last February and 2.4% last March. With tariff concerns and oil price drops, the Fed may keep the interest rate unchanged. I think this will provide Live Oak some breathing room to reprice loans and deposits and stabilize yields.

It has not yet hinted at the portion of loans for repricing. But we must note that 88% of its loans have variable rates. And I think these are well-positioned if we consider how LOB diversifies them. Its portfolio is concentrated on commercial loans, which are typically more secure. This makes LOB more shielded.

It may be confusing at first knowing that net charge offs rose and affected its net interest income. Yet, we must remember that its loan base rose by 24.6% in only a year. As we can see, it made a substantial but reasonable increase in loan allowances to account for delinquencies and defaults. And I think this is a conservative approach of the company despite its continued expansion.

Meanwhile, 3% of its deposits are non-interest-bearing. But half of its interest-bearing deposits are time deposits, which are typically fixed deposits. These can give LOB some flexibility to potential Fed rate changes. So even if uncertainty arises amid tariff and recession woes, this portion of deposits will remain unaffected. It is also important to note that only 2.2% of its deposits are demand deposits. This lowers deposit run risks.

Lastly, it keeps its loan-to-deposit ratio manageable at 87.0%, which is still within the ideal range of 80-90%. It still has adequate reserves to cover delinquencies. This proves LOB maintains a conservative approach to growth in the current market condition.

Well-Diversified Loan Portfolio

Aside from loan rates, I like its concentration on commercial loans. A substantial part of it is purely commercial banking for small businesses. It’s a prudent focus considering that SMBs comprise 99% of US businesses. This makes it easy for LOB to increase its loan origination and expand its customer base. The idea that a substantial portion of its loans and deposits have variable rates offers flexibility to borrowers and depositors.

In addition, its real estate exposure is also purely commercial. CRE loans comprise 39.5% of its total loans. Since these are mostly small businesses, LOB is geared more towards retail spaces. As we can see, retail properties have the most stable supply and demand. This means more stable property prices and mortgages. This also has the steadiest asset value and returns, making them desirable amid macroeconomic volatility. This is probably the reason its loan yields continue to increase as it expands despite the 1% rate cut in only a few months.

Another thing I like about commercial loans is that they are less interest-sensitive, so they are more stable. If the rate cut continues, the decrease in loan yields will be manageable. If the economy becomes more volatile, they can be handled well. After all, most of them have variable rates, so LOB can adjust them to market conditions.

Lastly, 31.6% of its loans are guaranteed. This means that the specific portion of its loans is backed by third parties, mainly the US Small Business Administration or the US SBA. This limits the risk of insolvency and gives it an extra capital buffer.

Well-Capitalized Portfolio

LOB has solid capital adequacy with an 11.0% CET1 Ratio. It means that LOB’s high-quality capital will cover 11.0% of its risk-weighted assets. Its Tier 1 Capital Ratio is the same, showing that all its capital is high quality. If we add the other capital, the Total Capital Ratio will increase to 12.3%. This can prove LOB’s adequate capacity to absorb potential losses without getting insolvent. Note that this is about 1.5x the minimum ratio of 8.0% required by Basel III. Hence, LOB can withstand a financial crisis or a recession even without resorting to borrowing. It also proves that LOB has enough capital buffer to prevent or at least minimize deposit run risks.

Price Ratios

At this point, we are certain that LOB has excellent fundamentals, positioning it well against macroeconomic volatility. So, we will also know whether LOB is worth the risk for its current valuation.

As a bank, a reliable metric to use is the P/B Ratio. However, I noticed that over the years, the price had not been too sensitive or responsive to it. If we trace back its movement, it was more consistent with the P/E Ratio. Before I assess the stock, I decided to analyze the price versus the EPS and BVPS using a regression analysis.

As you can see, there is an 86% correlation, showing their direct relationship. But using the p-value, the relationship between the EPS and the stock price is the significant one. It is only 0.0169, which means that only 1.69% of their relationship happened by chance. This can erase the randomness and bias of our observation. Meanwhile, the p-value of the BVPS is high at 0.268. This means that 26.8% of their data are random.

Despite this, I decided to use them both to compare the value of the stock relative to the previous years. They both show that LOB is much cheaper today than the previous years. If we focus on the P/E, LOB is trading at 13.9x its EPS, which is lower than the five-year average of 17.1x. This gives a TP of $28.83, a 22.7% upside potential. Likewise, the P/B shows underpricing. While the TP appears too much, the actual P/B Ratio today at 1.07x is more reasonable than the previous years.

Although these are historical numbers, we can get a clue about the price direction relative to these metrics, especially the EPS. The agreement between the statistical analysis and the P/E Ratio confirms its undervaluation.

Technicals

LOB has shown a weak momentum recently as both the price and the 50-day SMA fell below the 200-day SMA. Despite this, the flat 200-day SMA shows stability in its long-term trend. This also shows that the downtrend is due to negative sentiments recently. Technical indicators do not suggest an instant rebound as MACD remains in the negative region.

However, we can find buying opportunities at the current price for several reasons. First, the stock is already trading at its 52-week low, confirming its cheapness. Second, MACD’s histogram is still in the negative region, but it’s coming closer to the zero line. This can indicate a decreasing selling volume or momentum. Note that we already saw a bullish engulfing candle last April 9, showing that many buyers already saw new support at the $22-24 range. Lastly, the stock’s RSI is still in the oversold region, which can be a driver of its rebound.

As our trading strategy using a 1:1 Reward-Risk Ratio, take profit at $28.85. Coincidentally, this is almost identical to my P/E TP, giving logic to the recent trading behavior. Meanwhile, stop loss at $18.09.

Conclusion

Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. is a good addition to your portfolio as it still has room for expansion. Its strategic portfolio diversification and focus on commercial banking help it sustain its growth. In addition, it is still cheap with decent returns. Technicals are still weak, but a rebound must be considered. I am giving this stock a buy rating.

data.title: Boeing Gets Gut-Punched With Latest Move From China
data.description:
When the United States decided to begin the trade war that has started, my thought was not necessarily on short-term implications. Rather, it was on the long term impact that might occur because of our decision as a country to alienate our closest allies and trading partners. In conversations that I have had with those, I have often invoked Boeing (NYSE:BA) as an example of how the US could experience significant negative consequences in the long run. For years, the company has been a mess. Poor management, out of control costs, and other factors, have all negatively affected the enterprise. Even so, as of last year, the business had a roughly 40.6% market share in the airplane manufacturing industry when we are talking about major airplanes used for mass passenger travel. The other 60.4% of the market is controlled by Airbus SE (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY), which is based out of France.

With Boeing already experiencing issues, what might be the final nail in the coffin for the company would be if it becomes priced out of this industry or, even worse, if other countries just stop buying its aircrafts entirely. The collapse of this industrial behemoth would prove painful for all involved. Globally, the company has about 172,000 employees. Only 15% of those are located outside of the US. This could mean the loss of about 146,200 jobs in this country alone. This doesn't even factor in experience in relation to these because of the reliance of other enterprises on the firm.

At this time, I am not expecting Boeing to collapse. I have been, however, bearish about it as of late. In early March, I decided to downgrade the company to a 'sell' because of the problems it is facing. Since then, the stock is down 12.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 9.6%. But what has prompted me to revisit the firm so soon was news, on April 14th, that China has decided to stop taking delivery of new aircraft made by the enterprise. This is the latest sign of escalation between the US and China amidst this trade war. Even if these problems are temporary, this is still a bad situation for the company, especially considering the significance of China when it comes to global air travel and how that will grow over the next couple of decades. Because of this, I remain bearish about Boeing to the point of maintaining it as a 'sell'.

Boeing's situation just got worse

News reports began circulating on the evening of April 14th that China has decided to stop taking delivery of new aircraft produced by Boeing. Apparently, this will not apply to every single one of the aircraft that is going to China. According to reporting, the firm has 10 737 MAX aircraft that are currently positioned for handover to Chinese carriers. Any additional aircraft for which payments and delivery documents were finalized before April 12th might still be permitted to enter China, but it seems as though it will require approval from China's Civil Aviation Administration on a case-by-case basis.

In my previous article about the company, I provided an assessment of its operations more broadly. I don't think it would be useful to rehash those details. However, it is likely that this decision, if it extends for any meaningful window of time, will have a negative impact on the aircraft manufacturer. Asia has been one of the only growth areas for the company in recent years. As an example, we need only look at the firm's BCA segment, which is responsible for the development and sale of commercial jet aircraft such as the 737, 767, 777, and 787, as well as other programs such as the 777X.

Between 2022 and 2024, revenue for this segment dropped from $26.03 billion to $22.86 billion. Those two years did admittedly serve as bookends for the blockbuster year that was 2023 when the company generated $33.90 billion in revenue thanks to strong deliveries in Europe, the Middle East, and the US. By 2024, revenue generated from US customers was nearly halved from the $14.50 billion generated in 2023 to $7.33 billion last year. There were declines across all other major regions as well. The one exception to this was Asia. From 2022 to 2024, there has been consistent growth in that part of the world, with sales almost doubling from $4.49 billion to $8.06 billion. If we count in military sales as part of this equation, we would see revenue climb from $8.39 billion to $11.99 billion.

We don't exactly know the full impact that China alone has on the company. We do know that, at the end of last year, the firm had 55 737-8 aircraft in its inventory that were produced before 2023 that it owes to customers. 40 of these were dedicated to customers in China, and the company said that it expected to deliver all of them this year. In the past, there have been problems regarding Boeing and China. For instance, the company has not received a major order from a Chinese airline in multiple years, with some of that being because of highly publicized and reputationally damaging crashes of Boeing's 737 MAX aircraft in 2018 and 2019.

The longer these issues with trade persist, the more likely it will be that Boeing will be permanently affected. In August of last year, the company issued their own assessment about how big China's commercial fleet of aircraft can grow over the next couple of decades. They expect air travel in mainland China to grow about 5.2% per annum over the 20 years ending in 2043. This should create demand for 8,830 new aircraft over this time, with 60% of that involving a growing demand for air travel for the country, and the remaining 40% aimed at replacing older jets with more fuel-efficient models. This would all mean a more-than-doubling of the size of the commercial fleet in China over the 20 year period as the size of its commercial fleet expands from 4,345 to 9,740. That's a 4.1% annualized growth rate, with that expansion fueled by annual passenger traffic growth of 5.9% compared to the 4.7% that the globe on average should enjoy.

In order to accommodate this demand, Chinese carriers will need about $780 billion of aviation services, with examples including digital solutions, maintenance, and modifications. It will also open up the of opportunity when it comes to train pilots, maintenance technicians, cabin crew, and more. Boeing believes that the number of new personnel under these categories will grow by 430,000. This doesn't even factor in other ways that the company could be exposed to worsening conditions in China. Globally, Boeing has over 10,000 of its airplanes flying with parts that are made in China. And the activity generated by the company is responsible for about $1.5 billion in direct support for China's economy. This is not stuff that will just go away. Instead, it will be gobbled up by Airbus. Operational efficiencies that can be captured from continued growth by its largest competitor, a competitor that frankly is outperforming it, will only widen the gap between the two in terms of performance and opportunity.

Hopefully, investors will get some insight into the challenges that Boeing will face rather soon. This is because, before the market opens on April 23rd, management is expected to announce financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts have been mixed about the company. For instance, they expect revenue to total about $19.87 billion. This would mark a nice increase over the $16.57 billion the business reported one year earlier. This should be fueled in large part by a rise in deliveries that the company enjoyed. During the first quarter, we know that the firm delivered 130 commercial airplanes, including 105 of the 737 class. That's up from the 83 delivered the previous year. Year over year, it's also known that aircraft delivered for the Defense, Space, and Security programs for the company grew from 14 to 26.

Unfortunately, the bottom line for the company is expected to worsen. Earnings per share is expected to drop from a loss of $0.56 last year to a loss of $0.73 per share this year. That would mean a decline in net profits from negative $343 million to negative $472 million. On an adjusted basis, the net loss is expected to widen from $0.57 per share to $1.28 per share. That would mean a worsening from an adjusted net loss from $350 million to $828 million. In the table above, you can see other financial results for the first quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. In all likelihood, if profits and adjusted profits both worsen, these metrics will also decline year over year.

The good news is that while China poses a risk for the company at this time, the business does continue to land orders from other parts of Asia. On March 30th of this year, management announced that it received orders for 50 737 MAX jets courtesy of BOC Aviation, which operates as an airplane leasing business based out of Singapore. Earlier that month, Malaysia Aviation Group put in an order for up to 60 of the same aircraft. This will include 18 737-8 aircraft and 12 737-10 aircraft, with options for 30 more as Malaysia Airlines seeks to renew its single aisle fleet. Here at home, Boeing also announced on the same day that it had been selected by the US Air Force for a contract to design, build, and deliver its next-generation fighter aircraft.

Takeaway

As much as I want to be bullish about such an interesting and iconic company as Boeing, the fact of the matter is that there are plenty of reasons to be bearish at this time. The firm continues to deal with significant operational challenges. Add on top of this uncertainty regarding the trade situation with China and that country's decision to temporarily shut Boeing out from its market, and I believe that the 'sell' rating I assigned the company previously is justified. In the event that management provides an upbeat view of the company when it reports financial results in the coming days, my mindset could change. But it would take a rather significant revelation for that to occur.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fiserv: Still A Long-Term Winner Even Under Uncertainty
data.description:
I rate Fiserv, Inc. (NYSE:FI) as a buy even in a context of uncertainty due to the tariffs imposed by the Trump's administration, particularly if your horizon is the long haul. I wrote about Fiserv in August 2024 in the article "Fiserv: Its Aggressive Moves Are Giving It Advantages Over Competitors," in which I rated the stock as a buy given its big acquisition of First Data in 2019, opening significant long-term growth opportunities to capture new markets and the development of more verticals in each of these markets. Since then, the stock price has risen 28% considering the recent drop in the stock markets due to the uncertainties about the tariffs' impacts. However, we need to assess deeply how those tariffs can impact Fiserv's business model to understand why we could start adding shares right now. Thus, the catalyst here is that the market seems to not be fully aware of the advantageous position of Fiserv to capture the SMB's growth over the long haul. Let's see.

Context

Fiserv achieved good results in 2024 as the GAAP revenue grew 7% in FY 2024 YoY, GAAP EPS grew 13% YoY, and free cash flow achieved $5.2 billion which increased 34% YoY. Organic revenue growth was 16% YoY for FY 2024, and adjusted EPS increased 17% YoY in the same period. Taking the revenue GAAP breakdown, we have the following:

It's clear that the merchant segment is the business line that is growing the most in the last years. In the merchant segment, one of Fiserv's crown jewels, Clover, contributes around 15% of Fiserv's total revenues. Management expects Clover to generate $3.5 billion for FY 2025 and $4.5 billion for FY 2026, implying a 29% CAGR since March 2022.

Another source of growth is the value-added services (VAS) associated with Clover, which showed a penetration of 20% in Q4 2024, representing a growth from the 19% in Q4 2023. Management is very optimistic about VAS expecting to grow its penetration even further in the next years:

We also provided an outlook for value-added services, part of the key growth elements of the Clover solution with an expectation that value-added service penetration would hit 25% this year and 27% next year. We're right on track. We added three points last year, went from 19% to 22%.

Regarding the financial segment, it is keeping a relatively stable growth in FY 2024. Fiserv keeps working on developing more business with its client Desjardins Group, the largest financial cooperative in North America, which was signed in 2023. Desjardins signed with Fiserv to enhance its credit card program, among others.

There are more businesses underway in this segment as Fiserv established new partnerships with Target and Verizon to enhance issuer processing capabilities. More partnerships were signed by Fiserv in 2024 in this segment, like that of Walmart, which will let customers pay for goods and services at the store directly from their bank accounts; the other one was with DoorDash, which gets drivers to be paid in advance while accessing some banking services.

On the other hand, Fiserv does not stop its acquisition plans, as it continued doing so in 2024, after more than 150 since its IPO in 1984; indeed, Fiserv acquired Nextable, a cloud-based reservation, waitlist, and table management system that enables restaurants to manage an omnichannel guest arrival experience. This acquisition will broaden Clover's platform offering.

Also, Fiserv acquired Payfare, which is a payment and fintech firm that offers instant payout and banking solutions to gig economy workers; this acquisition will reinforce Fiserv's financial segment. Finally, Fiserv acquired a Dutch payment company, CCV, to expand the presence of Clover in Europe. The range of the amounts paid by these acquisitions is around $100 and $400 million.

In general, we are talking about a very aggressive company that delivers stable revenue growth being a real threat to any player in the same industry given its size, strong moat associated with its long-standing relationships with many financial institutions, and its aggressive strategy, which combined contribute to solidifying its strong moat.

There are lots of contributors to the future growth of Fiserv, but we need to focus on the most critical to understand Fiserv's promising growth prospects, being Clover, one of the main drivers.

Clover takes advantage of Fiserv's moat

Fiserv works with thousands of financial institutions, developing long-standing relationships with deep B2B integrations. As a result, it's way easier for Fiserv to continue developing new partnerships not only with new financial institutions but also with companies. For instance, given its focus on small businesses, particularly since the acquisition of First Data, Fiserv closed a deal to be partner with ADP (Automatic Data Processing), which is enriching the value-added services from Clover to its customers.

In this sense, ADP sells payroll and human resources solutions through an extensive network of existing clients, and now, given the new partnership with Fiserv, Clover can include those services offered by ADP in its already existing group of services to the small businesses, deepening Clover's relationships with its customers while expanding its offering.

In exchange, ADP can cross-sell payroll and HR tools to the SMB sector, gain distribution and integration opportunities through Clover's POS systems, take advantage of Fiserv's client base, particularly in banking and retail, and sell its solutions with Clover, First Data, and other Fiserv products. Fiserv follows similar synergies through acquisitions or partnerships such as those with BentoBox, Tripleseat, Syncrony, First West Credit Union, etc.

As a result of looking for these synergies aggressively, Clover benefits from expanding its presence, new services, and innovations offered through its platform:

We can see in the chart above that Clover's main competitor, Block (XYZ), is decelerating its revenue growth and is far from that of Clover. This can help us to understand that Fiserv has a very hard-to-penetrate moat by competitors, as Fiserv's network is deeply integrated, through B2B integrations, with thousands of financial institutions, generating high switching costs for those financial institutions.

This strong and broader global payment network infrastructure is what enables Fiserv to establish more and more partnerships with other companies to reinforce its market position in the financial segment and in the merchant segment from which Clover is part. None of the other players within the payment processor industry has this strong network in the financial system that backs up their operations like in Fiserv's case to support in other segments like the merchant business line.

Also, there are other factors more associated with the entire sector of the POS systems in the merchant sector globally that will boost the demand for these devices and all the value-added services associated with it in the next years:

In the chart above, we can see that only 29% of the POS hardware is less than two years old, which means that 71% is older than two years, including 38% older than 5 years. This means that a renovation cycle of the POS systems would come in the next years with more associated value-added services. In addition, we can see a similar pattern in other devices of the POS system, such as mobile devices for stores and mobile devices.

The same study mentions that the global market value of the POS industry was at $13.7 billion in 2023, and it's expected to reach $30 billion in 2032, which means a growth of 9% CAGR for the next years.

In this sense, I think that Fiserv will keep growing its merchant segment through Clover and all the solutions associated with it, but also the company will keep developing more services and solutions to SMBs outside Clover's platform, as Fiserv's CFO pointed out in December 2024:

We expect that non-Clover business to generally grow mid-single digits globally. And we continue to sell it. We continue to see growth in existing clients using -- as our small businesses grow, so does our revenue, and we continue to sell it. Obviously, we believe Clover is a great solution, but it isn't necessarily the solution for all small businesses.

Not every small business needs to have a full-blown operating system. Some just like running their small business. The example I love to give is I'm running a pizzeria because I love making pies. I got five tabletops. I don't need a fancy full-blown operating system. Having a terminal on my counter is more than fine, and we're happy to sell that service to those clients.

As such, Fiserv is working to expand its merchant and financial segments through new partnerships and more acquisitions while developing new verticals, mainly in the solutions offered to the SMB sector, including Clover and non-Clover solutions, generating high switching costs for its clients and strengthening its moat over time.

Fiserv's financial indicators show that its strategy is working

I built a table that summarizes different key financial indicators that will show us the changes experienced by Fiserv in the last years and its moat:

I showed the same table in my last article, but I updated it, including the numbers of FY 2024. You can see that Fiserv's revenue growth was only between 2% and 4% before the First Data acquisition. Fiserv paid around $22 billion for First Data in an all-stock transaction, which included $17 billion of First Data's total debt.

As such, the table showed how Fiserv's total debt increased substantially in 2019 with that acquisition; however, we can notice that the acquisition is being successful when the FCF keeps growing, revenue growth increased significantly, and FCF margins were kept as high as they were before that big acquisition, which is remarkable. The returns on capital, ROE, and ROIC were impacted negatively given the higher level of the equity and total debt, respectively, as a result of the First Data acquisition, but both ratios are growing, making Fiserv's growth more profitable over the years.

The share buyback program is consistent and strong over the years thanks to a stable and growing FCF. This consistency is particularly important under scenarios of stress, as we are experiencing now, since Fiserv might be purchasing more of its own shares when the stock price is falling as a result of the current uncertainty associated with the Trump administration policies.

I explained in my previous article that First Data was so key to Fiserv not only for the size of the acquisition but also for the sort of subsidiaries that were part of First Data, such as Blue Pay, Card Connect, Acculynk, Transaction Wireless, Gyft, and its most innovative one, Clover. So, I would say that it was a brilliant move to buy First Data even when it seems rather risky given the size of the transaction and knowing that most acquisitions of this size are way more difficult to integrate.

Thanks to the acquisition of First Data, Fiserv could offer a wider range of services to more large banks and merchants, which, in my view, served well as a very strong platform to develop more new businesses for the existing clients and for new ones; in other words, more future growth for the long haul, which is music for a long-term investor.

In my view, First Data has made Fiserv a very strong player in the industry at the same notch as companies like Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) and stronger than PayPal (PYPL), Block, and Shift4 Payments (FOUR) in terms of quality, resiliency, and growth prospects.

Valuation

According to the valuation table offered by Seeking Alpha, Fiserv stock seems a bit expensive given its overall "D"; there are other multiples showing C, C+, or C-, which indicate that the stock price is fair:

However, sometimes multiples do not give us a clear picture about how cheap certain stocks are, particularly when the company is a high-quality one, so I want to use a DCF approach to complement the valuation system given by Seeking Alpha, so I will start with the assumptions.

Assumptions

  • Outstanding shares: 581,970,260.

  • FCF margins: 20.36% (the average of the last 5 years since the acquisition of First Data in 2019).

  • Revenue growth: 6.7% for 2025, 6% for 2026 (less than consensus).

  • Cash as of December 2024: $1,236 million.

  • Debt as of December 2024: $23,730 million.

  • Discount rate: 9%.

  • FCF growth in perpetuity: 7% annual (FCF growth from 2019 to 2024: 16% CAGR). This assumption is less than half the growth of FCF of 16% CAGR since the First Data acquisition in 2019. This figure should be taken with a long-term horizon.

To find the perpetuity, we used the formula:

Perpetuity = FCF 2027/(discounted rate - g).

where g = FCF growth in perpetuity, which was assumed to be 7% annually.

With perpetuity, we calculate the present value of all the FCFs beyond 2027. Then, we calculate the enterprise value using the following:

Enterprise Value = Present Value of FCF (from 2025 to 2026) + Perpetuity + Cash - Total Debt.

Finally, the intrinsic value is calculated by taking the enterprise value and dividing it by the outstanding number of shares. In this way, we could get $258 per share under the assumptions presented, which is an improvement from the $189 per share in my last article, given the fact that the management has demonstrated a solid execution to keep growing the business through different growth avenues while reinforcing the competitive advantages.

Risks

I think that the main risk here is how the trade war between China and the US can impact Fiserv's business model. Fiserv's source of revenues is concentrated in North America, specifically in the US and Canada.

As we can see in the table above, the US and Canada concentrate 85% of Fiserv's total revenues in the last years, whereas only 15% come from outside those countries. Now, Fiserv's risk is not associated with the place where it manufactures its POS systems and other devices since all of them are manufactured in the US; however, there are components that are imported from other countries.

As such, tariffs could increase the price of a group of components of those POS systems and devices, particularly if those are imported from China, which can end up pushing up Fiserv's costs of manufacturing; for instance, microprocessors, memory chips, touchscreens, card readers, PINs, etc. Second, tariffs can also impact Fiserv's business through the increase in the price of different inputs that might affect the demand of its client's services, particularly in the merchant segment where it's included restaurants, stores, etc.

Finally, tariffs can also impact Fiserv's business through a general reduction of the consumption rates in the economy. I found the following in the risk factors of Fiserv's 10-K:

Potential tariffs or trade wars could increase the cost of our products, which could adversely impact the competitiveness of our products and our financial results.

The U.S. has imposed tariffs, and may impose new or increased tariffs, on certain imports from other countries, which may lead to retaliatory tariffs imposed by other governments. If the U.S. administration or other countries impose new or increased tariffs, trade restrictions or restrictions on the cross-border flow of data, our manufacturing of hardware devices, supply of raw materials and access to certain markets, could be impacted. Although it is difficult to predict how current or future tariffs on items imported from other countries will impact our business, the cost of our products manufactured in other countries and imported into the U.S. or elsewhere could increase, which could adversely affect the demand for these products and have a material adverse effect on our business and results of operations.

Under this scenario, Fiserv might transfer part of the cost to its clients but not 100%. In addition, most likely Fiserv might look for components from countries with the least tariffs that replace components from China, such as South Korea and India, among other regions renowned for electronics production.

In regard to the tariffs impacting the business from Fiserv's clients, particularly in the merchant segment, Fiserv can support its clients by offering more new solutions that help them save money. Fiserv is already offering solutions such as Cash Flow Central, which is an account payable/accounts receivable solution that enables small businesses to manage their cash flows more efficiently.

In addition, under a higher-tariff scenario, Fiserv can even raise prices in the enterprise segment to avoid significantly impacting the SMB sector, which has more sensitivity to the economic cycles and abrupt changes from the international economic scenario.

In the end, there will be adjustments by manufacturers transferring assets outside of China, or other suppliers in other geographies will experience an increased demand for their components, given that their locations will not be punished by higher tariffs as China. That's why Fiserv's stock should be seen as a long-term holding, as those adjustments can take place gradually, enabling Fiserv to keep growing over the long term.

Another risk is the total debt, which seems a bit high due to the acquisition of First Data in 2019. However, in 2019, the total debt/FCF was 10x, and the same ratio has been gradually declining to reach 4.9x in FY 2024, so it seems like the management is not planning to reduce the debt but dilute it with the growth of the business, so it would not be a surprise to see the ratio total debt/FCF ratio of 1x or 2x in the next years as the business is growing profitably.

Finally, CEO Frank Bisignano, who was responsible for Fiserv's good performance in the last 5 years, was replaced by Michael P. Lyons on January 2025. Bisignano has been nominated by President Donald Trump as commissioner of the Social Security Administration; in this sense, some investors felt that Lyon's performance might not be as good as Bisignano's, but it seems like there is lots of confidence in Lyon's appointment given his experience in mergers, acquisitions, and digital payments positions.

Takeaways

I find the stock a way better choice than companies like PayPal or Block as a long-term investment. Fiserv took a high risk buying First Data in 2019 since a very large acquisition used to increase substantially the debt levels, the goodwill on the balance sheet, and risks associated with integration and synergies.

However, after that acquisition, Fiserv was able to integrate very interesting operating systems like Clover to be part of Fiserv's portfolio of products, and the company's performance in the last years through different metrics shown in this article seems to indicate that Fiserv passed the exam.

Fiserv did what needed to be done with that acquisition to secure long-term growth not only in the US, which represents 85% of its total revenues, but also in fast-growing markets like Latin America with lots of room for growth, and now new geographies like Australia, Singapore, etc., where Clover has so many opportunities to keep growing according to the chart above.

In this article, I focused more on the Clover development in Fiserv's merchant segment, as I expect a very good growth in the segment for the next years, but other solutions oriented to the world's largest businesses in the world through the CARAT solutions within the merchant segment for enterprises, such as omnichannel payment processing, fraud mitigation, data as a service, embedded financial solutions, etc., are also poised to deliver stable and predictable growth for the next years.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Stock With A Catalyst Idea investment competition, which runs through April 17. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Fixed-Income Update: Bonds Reclaim Role As A Volatility Buffer
data.description:
By Gene Tannuzzo, CFA

Global capital markets have seen a noticeable shift in expectations this year. Although backward-looking economic fundamentals still look solid — unemployment is low, inflation has moderated and corporate fundamentals are generally strong — investors are feeling more cautious. The optimism that peaked following the U.S. election has given way to concerns over downside risks to growth. Markets have responded to this shift with equities struggling, credit spreads widening and Treasury yields falling.

Entering 2025, investors expected pro-growth policies (deregulation and tax cuts) to take center stage. Instead, the focus has been on tariffs and tighter immigration policies — both of which could weigh on growth. The Federal Reserve (Fed) has also acknowledged that risks are skewing to the downside.

As investors grapple with these challenges, fixed income has regained its traditional role as an effective portfolio diversifier. In this environment, a focus on high-quality bonds and selective credit exposure is essential.

Drivers of bond market returns

Several factors have shaped bond market performance so far this year:

  • Softer U.S. growth expectations: Economic growth in 2024 was solid, but concerns are rising about headwinds in 2025. Geopolitical risks, policy uncertainty and weakening business and consumer sentiment have shaken investor confidence.
  • Inflation uncertainty: While the inflation surge of 2022 has largely faded, the potential impact of new tariffs looms large. With recency bias likely playing a role, investors are wary of a scenario where inflation reaccelerates, which could keep the Fed on hold longer than currently expected.
  • Government spending and pro-growth policies outside the U.S.: The U.S. bond market typically drives bond yields globally, but in recent weeks, global developments have been impacting U.S. markets. Notably, expectations for fiscal stimulus and other pro-growth policies in Germany and Japan have exerted upward pressure on U.S. rates.

So far this year, credit has underperformed duration. Despite this weakness, credit markets have remained orderly, with buyers stepping in to capitalize on improved valuations. Today’s modestly wider spreads reflect lower Treasury yields rather than outright weakness in corporate bond prices. As expected in this environment, high-quality fixed-rate debt has outperformed high-yield, bank loans and other floating-rate instruments. Overall, however, credit is still fairly expensive by historical standards.

A surprising source of outperformance, relative to expectations at the beginning of 2025, has been emerging markets. Investors in emerging market debt have already navigated similar tariff policies during the previous Trump administration, and risk premiums were built in last fall. Importantly, tariff discussions have been equally focused on developed market trading partners like Europe and Canada, as they have been on emerging market economies like China and Mexico.

What’s next for the Fed and rates?

The Fed held rates steady at its latest meeting but announced a slowdown in the pace of its quantitative tightening program. This suggests a subtle shift toward easing, even if rate cuts aren’t imminent.

The Fed faces a tricky balancing act. Tariffs could push inflation higher, but they could also hurt growth by reducing consumer spending and business investment. This interplay adds another layer of complexity to the Fed’s decision-making process.

The Fed has indicated that it remains open to cutting rates twice this year. Before committing to a path, it will likely need to see clearer signs of how the potential tug of war between rising inflation and weakening demand will play out.

Positioning portfolios in this environment

As credit spreads have widened, pockets of opportunity have emerged in sectors with less exposure to trade and growth risks. Security selection will be important here, as certain industries — such as autos and building materials — face greater pressure from tariffs and supply chain disruptions, while others, like chemicals and some consumer sectors, may hold up better. We think dispersion within the high-yield market is likely to grow, and credit selection will be a significant driver of returns.

One of the key themes this year is the return of fixed income as a portfolio diversifier. Unlike recent years, when bonds struggled to provide downside protection, high-quality fixed income has reasserted its role as a volatility buffer. With equity markets facing ongoing uncertainty, this dynamic is likely to remain in focus.

The bottom line

Investors should be prepared for a fluid economic outlook as the year unfolds. Growth concerns are rising, policy uncertainty remains high and the Fed’s next moves will be closely scrutinized. In this environment, a nuanced, diversified approach to portfolio construction will be essential.

Disclosures

There are risks associated with fixed-income investments, including credit risk, interest rate risk, and prepayment and extension risk. In general, bond prices rise when interest rates fall and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer-term securities.
